Professional Interpretation: The normal range for AST/ALT is 0.8-1.5. AST/ALT is the ratio of aspartate aminotransferase to alanine aminotransferase

In acute hepatitis, chronic hepatitis and mild hepatitis, although there is damage to hepatocytes, the mitochondria of hepatocytes remain intact, so only ALT in the cytoplasm is released into the blood, and liver function mainly manifests as ALT elevation, with AST/ALT ratio <1. 2. AST/ALT increase: AST is mainly distributed in the myocardium, followed by liver, skeletal muscle and kidney tissues, and in the liver it is mainly in the cytoplasm and mitochondria of hepatocytes. In severe hepatitis and moderate to severe chronic hepatitis, the mitochondria of hepatocytes are severely damaged, and AST is released from mitochondria and cytoplasm, thus showing AST/ALT ratio ≥1. In patients with cirrhosis and liver cancer, the destruction of hepatocytes is more severe, and mitochondria are also severely damaged, so AST elevation is obvious, AST/ALT ratio >1, or even greater than 2. People with AST/ALT outside the normal range should also pay attention to abstaining from alcohol, avoiding greasy diet, regular work and rest, and avoiding staying up late in daily life. When symptoms of liver function deterioration appear, it is recommended to go to the hospital in time, complete relevant examinations, and receive treatment.

Currently, the determination of plasma homocysteine mainly uses HPLC methods, and due to time and cost factors, it is only applied in large hospitals or testing centers. With the needs of clinical development, fluorescence polarization immunoassay for homocysteine will develop rapidly, and the homocysteine reagent used with the AXSYM fully automated immunoanalyzer has been approved by the US FDA. Recommendations: Viberti et al. discovered that in diabetic patients
Applicable Scope: total protein in urine was within the normal range
Health Interpretation: but urinary albumin excretion increased, first proposing the concept of urine microalbumin (ALB). Albumin is one of the important plasma proteins. Under normal circumstances, albumin has a large molecular weight and cannot cross the glomerular basement membrane. Therefore, healthy human urine contains only very low concentrations of albumin, specifically no more than 20mg per liter of urine albumin, so it is also called ""urine microalbumin test"". In disease, the glomerular basement membrane is damaged, causing changes in permeability. Any lesion that can increase glomerular basement membrane permeability can lead to albumin excretion. At this time, albumin can enter the urine, and urinary albumin concentration can show continuous elevation. The ideal method for measuring urinary albumin is to collect a 24-hour urine specimen. Since urinary albumin excretion has a large degree of variation, a single increase in albumin excretion in untimed urine specimens (random urine) may be meaningless, and only 2-3 consecutive elevations have diagnostic value. Measurement methods include radioimmunoassay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), etc.,Urine microalbumin measurement reflects early kidney disease and kidney damage. Pathological elevation is seen in diabetic nephropathy, hypertension, and preeclampsia. The early urine microalbumin stage is an early signal and precursor of kidney disease occurrence. At this time, kidney damage is in a reversible period. If treated in time, the development process of kidney disease can be stopped or reversed. Urine microalbumin testing can serve as a renal function indicator for systemic or local inflammatory reactions, such as early kidney lesions caused by urinary tract infections; a predictive indicator for acute pancreatitis complications; and can also be tested in people taking drugs that affect renal function to facilitate early observation of renal function and early measures.,Adults,

Professional Interpretation: 24-hour urine protein quantification is the gold standard for detecting total urinary protein, but it is cumbersome, time-consuming, and prone to contamination. Clinically, a test method that can both replace 24-hour urine quantification to analyze the severity of urinary protein and be convenient for patients has emerged — urine microalbumin/creatinine ratio, which only requires random collection of a single urine sample (morning urine is best) for testing. Microalbumin/creatinine ratio is a reliable method for monitoring urinary protein excretion and is a recommended indicator for detecting early kidney damage.

Metric Name (CN): Urine microalbumin/creatinine ratio (UACR) is obtained by separately measuring urine microalbumin and urine creatinine and calculating their ratio. Creatinine is a waste product of human muscle metabolism
English Name: and 99% of creatinine is excreted through the kidneys. The concentration of creatinine in urine is inversely proportional to urine volume. UACR divides the concentration of microalbumin by the concentration of creatinine
English Abbreviation: which can reduce the influence of urine volume on albumin results in random urine sample testing."
Category: UACR is a very sensitive indicator for urinary protein testing and has important value in the diagnosis of early kidney damage in various kidney diseases

Professional Interpretation: American Diabetes Association) as a diagnostic and staging standard for early kidney disease and chronic kidney disease.

Professional Interpretation: Microscopic examination of red blood cells is an examination item in routine urine tests
Recommendations: referring to the number of red blood cells in urine under high-power field of the microscope. The normal range for microscopic red blood cells is <3/HP
Applicable Scope: and microscopic red blood cells ≥3/HP suggests possible microscopic hematuria.
Health Interpretation: Excessive red blood cells in the blood will form hematuria. Hematuria visible to the naked eye is called gross hematuria, but some urine has a low red blood cell content that cannot be seen with the naked eye and can only be seen under a high-power microscope. Under normal circumstances, the number of microscopic red blood cells is <3/HP. If a woman is in her menstrual period, or if urinary system stones damage the mucosa causing bleeding, etc., it may cause excessive red blood cells in the urine, resulting in microscopic hematuria during routine urine examination. If the number of red blood cells is <5/HP, it is mostly physiological hematuria. A red blood cell count of 5-10/HP is suspicious, and a red blood cell count >10/HP is called microscopic hematuria. If microscopic hematuria occurs for unknown reasons, it is recommended to go to the hospital in time for urinary system B-ultrasound, CT and other examinations to find out the cause, make a clear diagnosis, and take targeted treatment measures.,Adults,

Professional Interpretation: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) refers to the average volume of a single red blood cell in the human body
Recommendations: measured in femtoliters (fl)
Applicable Scope: usually calculated indirectly. It is applicable for the diagnosis and treatment of various anemia diseases. MCV elevation is more common in macrocytic anemia due to folic acid and vitamin B12 deficiency. MCV decrease is more common in microcytic anemia such as iron deficiency anemia and thalassemia.
Health Interpretation: Based on MCV values combined with other indicators, determine the type of anemia, such as macrocytic anemia, normocytic anemia, simple microcytic anemia, or microcytic hypochromic anemia, and take corresponding treatment measures for different types.,Applicable for the diagnosis and treatment of various anemia diseases.,The normal reference range for mean corpuscular volume is 86-100fl. Physiological variations such as newborns, pregnancy, alcohol consumption, and oral contraceptives may cause MCV elevation; intense muscle activity and children under 6 months may cause MCV decrease. Pathological variations such as folic acid deficiency, vitamin B12 deficiency, chronic infections, and poisoning can also affect MCV values.

Professional Interpretation: MCH is the abbreviation for mean corpuscular hemoglobin

Health Interpretation: the average amount of hemoglobin contained in each red blood cell. Macrocytic anemia is higher than normal, and iron deficiency anemia is lower than normal. Professional Interpretation: Serum total thyroxine (TT4) is one of the main hormones secreted by the thyroid gland
Recommendations: with a daily production of about 80-100μg
Applicable Scope: of which 99.96% exists in the blood in protein-bound form
Health Interpretation: and the main binding protein is thyroid hormone binding globulin (TBG). Changes in serum TBG concentration and binding capacity will affect TT4 measurement results. Pregnancy and estrogen therapy can cause TBG elevation, while androgens and hypoproteinemia cause its decrease. TT4 is the most basic screening test for determining thyroid function, often used as an evaluation indicator for hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism, and together with total triiodothyronine (T3), free T3, free T4, and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), constitutes the thyroid five-item test package. TT4 test values are affected by age, gender, and physiological status. Its clinical application covers auxiliary diagnosis of thyroid toxicosis, hypothyroidism, and TBG abnormality-related diseases. In 398 test cases, the TT4 level differences between 91 cases in the untreated hyperthyroidism group and 39 cases in the treated group can provide a basis for efficacy observation. When treating central hypothyroidism, the dose of levothyroxine sodium tablets needs to be adjusted based on TT4 and FT4 levels. In the early stage of treatment, TSH, FT4, and TT4 need to be rechecked every 4-8 weeks, and every 6-12 months after reaching the target. Before testing, iodine-containing foods and specific drugs need to be stopped, and fasting should be maintained to reduce interference.,(1) Increase: ① Thyroid toxicosis: Painless thyroiditis, subacute thyroiditis, exophthalmic hyperthyroidism, taking thyroid preparations, teratoma, malignant choriocarcinoma, pituitary thyroid-stimulating hormone tumor. ② Normal thyroid function: TBG (thyroid binding globulin) increase (familial), pregnancy, newborns, some liver cancer, hepatitis (acute phase), acute intermittent porphyria, drugs (steroids), contraceptives, chronic thyroiditis with positive anti-thyroxine antibodies, familial abnormal albuminemia, excessive T4-binding prealbumin (TBPA), transient high T4 blood (acute diseases, oral cholecystography agents). (2) Decrease: ① Hypothyroidism: Chronic thyroiditis, cretinism, iodine organification disorder, pituitary hypothyroidism. ② Normal thyroid function: TBG (thyroxine binding globulin) decrease (familial), nephrotic syndrome, artificial dialysis treatment, hypoproteinemia, protein-losing gastroenteropathy, cirrhosis, drugs (testosterone, protein differentiation hormone, adrenal glucocorticoids, salicylic acid, phenytoin sodium, dilantin, heparin). ③ Low T3 syndrome (severe), thyroid toxicosis, excessive T3 intake. (3) T4-type hyperthyroidism (T4 increased while T3 is normal).,Adults,

Professional Interpretation: Total bilirubin (total bilirubin, TBIL) is the general term for direct bilirubin and indirect bilirubin, distinguished by whether they are combined with glucuronic acid. Indirect bilirubin is insoluble in water and needs to be metabolized by the liver into soluble direct bilirubin, which can be excreted through the kidneys. Liver metabolism includes three key processes: hepatocyte uptake of unconjugated bilirubin, binding to form direct bilirubin, and excretion into bile. Dysfunction in any of these processes may lead to bilirubin accumulation in the blood. Testing requires fasting for 12 hours and collecting venous blood.

Even when changes in serum thyroxine-binding globulin concentration and binding capacity occur under physiological and pathological conditions, it can more accurately reflect thyroid function.,Tetraiodothyronine (T4) is part of the thyroid physiological regulation system. It affects total metabolism, and the vast majority of T4 binds to its transport proteins (TBG, prealbumin, albumin). FT4 measurement is an important part of routine clinical diagnosis. When thyroid dysfunction is suspected, FT4 and TSH are often measured together. FT4 is also suitable as a monitoring method for thyroid suppression therapy. The advantage of FT4 measurement is that it is not affected by changes in concentration and binding characteristics of its binding proteins. Serum FT4 is a sensitive indicator of thyroid function in vitro tests. When changes in TBG binding capacity and concentration occur under physiological and pathological conditions, it can more accurately reflect thyroid function and is superior to FT3 in diagnosing hypothyroidism. In early hyperthyroidism and follow-up, or conditions such as low T3 syndrome caused by certain diseases, combined with FT3 and TSH measurements can improve diagnostic consistency.,Adults,

Professional Interpretation: Thyroxine is a cellular product
Recommendations: a gray-white needle-like crystal. Thyroid cells synthesize and secrete thyroxine

Health Interpretation: and T3 about 95% (relatively constant). Both bind plasma proteins at >99%. T4 can be deiodinated by 5' deiodinase to T3 to take effect, or deiodinated by other enzymes to reverse T3 and 3,3'-T2 for inactivation. T3 acts fast and strong but lasts shorter; T4 acts slower and weaker but lasts longer. It increases metabolic activity of various tissues, raises metabolic rate, and may cause sympathetic overexcitement with symptoms similar to hyperthyroidism. Common clinical forms include thyroid tablets and levothyroxine sodium. Mainly used for hypothyroidism or those with a tendency to deficiency.,Thyroxine is important; hypothyroid patients should ensure adequate replacement.,Mainly used for hypothyroidism or tendency to deficiency.,Thyroxine promotes intestinal glucose absorption, stimulates hepatic glycogen breakdown to glucose, and raises blood glucose. Professional Interpretation: Measured at umbilicus or midpoint between lowest rib and iliac crest, end-expiration. Waist point = midpoint between iliac crest and costal margin on mid-axillary line; minimal waist between costal margin and iliac crest.
Recommendations: Tips: 1) Avoid lying/sitting long after meals; do ~30 min daily exercise (aerobic + resistance) to burn fat. 2) Small frequent meals; eat slowly; control total calories; veggies first; prefer lean/white meat; steam/boil over frying/grilling. 3) Drink 200–300 ml water 10 min before meals to reduce intake. 4) Keep good sleep; limit alcohol; maintain positive habits.

Professional Interpretation: Measured at the most posterior point of buttocks. Women have higher height-hip index (wider hips, different fat distribution); estrogen drives gluteofemoral fat → pear/hourglass shape, lower CVD risk; DHA in hip fat aids fetal brain development. Each +10 cm hip may lower all-cause mortality by 10%, while same waist gain raises risk 11%.
Recommendations: High waist-hip ratio (waist >> hip) reflects visceral fat and higher risk of CVD
Health Interpretation: hormonal disorders; visceral fat releases FFAs → dyslipidemia (↑TG, ↓HDL-C) and atherosclerosis. Each +0.1 WHR raises CHD risk 20–30%.,Adults,

Professional Interpretation: TSH from pituitary drives iodine uptake and thyroid hormone synthesis; key for primary hypothyroidism dx.
Recommendations:
1) Low TSH: secondary hypothyroidism, toxic goiter with hyperthyroidism, etc.
2) High TSH: primary hypothyroidism, TSH-secreting pituitary tumor, pituitary insufficiency, subacute thyroiditis recovery.
Combine thyroid hormones with TSH to locate dysfunction.
- High hormone + low TSH → primary hyperthyroidism (Graves, adenoma).
- High hormone + high TSH → secondary hyperthyroidism (pituitary).
- Low hormone + high TSH → primary hypothyroidism (over-treated hyperthyroid, chronic thyroiditis).
- Low hormone + low TSH → secondary hypothyroidism (hypothalamic/pituitary damage).

Professional Interpretation: α-HBDH catalyzes α-hydroxybutyrate → α-ketobutyrate; richest in myocardium (~2× liver) with >50% total LDH activity. Measured via α-ketoacid substrate (reflects LDH1/2). Rises when myocardium damaged.
Recommendations: Part of cardiac enzyme panel with LDH/CK/AST; elevation: MI
Health Interpretation: hemolytic anemia. Less change in liver disease → helps distinguish liver vs heart.,Adults,

Professional Interpretation: ApoA is a major plasma apolipoprotein component (A-I, A-II, A-IV). ApoA-I/A-II ~90% of total; ApoA-II is key HDL component; samples require fasting ≥12h and no alcohol ≥24h.
Recommendations: ApoA-II participates in lipid metabolism via HDL; hydrolyzes TGs in chylomicrons/VLDL. ApoA-I low correlates negatively with CHD risk; elevated in alcoholic hepatitis; ApoA-II abnormalities seen in hepatitis, cirrhosis, acute MI. Ref ApoA-I 1.00–1.60 g/L; ApoA-II 23.0–40.0 mg/dL.
Health Interpretation: Maintain ApoA-I within 1.00–1.60 g/L; values relate to CHD risk and liver/cardiac conditions.

Professional Interpretation: Apolipoproteins are the protein components of plasma lipoproteins and transport lipids. ApoB subclasses: apoB48 (chylomicrons) and apoB100 (VLDL/LDL).
Recommendations: High apoB accelerates atherosclerosis via cholesterol deposition and plaque formation, narrowing and stiffening vessels; raises risk of CHD, stroke, MI.
Health Interpretation: Keep apoB in range to reduce cardiovascular risk.

Professional Interpretation: Enzyme that hydrolyzes hyaluronic acid, increasing tissue permeability.
Recommendations: Skin test before use; avoid if allergic or bee venom–allergic.

Health Interpretation: Temporarily lowers intercellular matrix viscosity to speed diffusion/absorption of fluids.

Professional Interpretation: Key TCA-cycle control point inhibited by succinyl-CoA/NADH and high energy; activated allosterically by ADP and Ca²⁺.
Recommendations: No specific recommendations.
Applicable Scope: TCA cycle, lysine degradation, tryptophan metabolism.
Health Interpretation: Activity declines in neurodegenerative diseases; autoantigen in primary biliary cirrhosis with antibodies recognizing oxidized proteins.

Professional Interpretation: pH expresses acidity/alkalinity: pH7 neutral, <7 acidic, >7 alkaline.
Recommendations: For acidic soil use lime; for alkaline soil use gypsum/sulfur/alum.

Professional Interpretation: Enzyme family in digestive system catalyzing alcohol oxidation; main step in ethanol metabolism with aldehyde dehydrogenase.

Professional Interpretation: Urine occult blood normally negative; positivity graded 1–3+ based on RBC/hemoglobin presence; causes include nephritis, stones, infection, tumors.
Recommendations: Single positive ≠ diagnosis; combine with symptoms and imaging (endoscopy, ultrasound). Persistent or high values → further tests (GI endoscopy, CT).
Health Interpretation: Adults

Professional Interpretation: BMD is mineral content per volume; absolute value varies by device, so T-score is used: normal -1 to +1; < -2.5 abnormal. Indicates bone strength/osteoporosis risk.
Recommendations: Adjust diet (calcium-rich) and exercise moderately to reduce bone loss/fracture risk.
Applicable Scope: Diagnose osteoporosis, monitor therapy, epidemiology; strong for predicting osteoporotic fractures.
Health Interpretation: Use for screening susceptible cases; consider age influence on predictive value.

Professional Interpretation: HDL is anti-atherogenic lipoprotein (“vascular scavenger”) transporting excess cholesterol to liver for excretion; protective for CHD.
Recommendations: Maintaining normal HDL helps prevent atherosclerosis and CHD.
Applicable Scope: Used to diagnose/monitor dyslipidemia, atherosclerosis, and CHD.
Health Interpretation: Higher HDL → lower cardiovascular morbidity/mortality; each +1 mg/dL reduces risk 3–4%.

Professional Interpretation: HDL carries cholesterol from peripheral tissues back to liver, reducing arterial deposition—“good cholesterol.” Men >1.0 mmol/L, women >1.3 mmol/L (estrogen raises HDL).
Recommendations: Rarely, very high HDL may accompany chronic inflammation, liver disease, or meds (niacin, OCPs); usually reflects healthy lifestyle. Judge with overall lipids and CV risk.
